questions.text: I find that I am usually focused on the content of a conversation rather than its politics.
I hold the view that the best friendships are the ones where I give more than I get.
I detect when a person uses the phrase 'no offense' as a signal that offense is exactly what they intend.
I experience the onset of emotion as a sudden physical sensation before I can even name what I'm feeling.
I believe that the obligation to respond to suffering is a moral imperative whose force does not weaken with repetition or familiarity.
I tend to make major life decisions based primarily on what excites and fulfills me.
I find that maintaining a personal journal focused on my own progress is deeply satisfying.
I would rather make a bold, imperfect decision than let the group stall.
I am attracted to opportunities where I can set the tone for a team.
I feel that the moral weight of suffering should be assigned based on its severity, not on how emotionally compelling the person's story is.
I hold the view that putting others first is simply the right way to live.
I notice expired items when I happen to come across them, not on a set schedule.
I rarely detect when someone's reaction to news is performed for the benefit of the person sharing it.
I pick up on when a silence after a question is the asker giving space versus the asker applying pressure.
I hold the view that true freedom means not being bound by predetermined plans.
I break vague goals into concrete next actions so I always know what to do next.
I tend to accept only the projects that align with my personal vision.
I sense when a seemingly casual question is actually loaded with significance.
I gravitate toward letting discussions continue until the group is satisfied.
I believe in measuring what matters so I can manage it effectively.
I feel that I respond to the world with a kind of natural equanimity.
I would rather participate in a panel discussion than give a keynote alone.
I think that the most equitable decisions come from those who can weigh competing claims of suffering without being pulled toward the most emotionally compelling one.
I feel that planning for worst-case scenarios wastes energy better spent on execution.
I believe that the moral weight I give to others' suffering is not a burden but a compass.
I am drawn to moments where one person's decision changes everything.
I offer my expertise gently and only when it seems helpful, not as an assertion of authority.
When I receive unexpected praise, the flush of pleasure is so strong it makes me lightheaded.
I plan vacations and trips well in advance, with itineraries and reservations.
I find that I can sit with uncomfortable news without being destabilized by it.
I rarely feel the kind of emotional urgency that demands immediate action.
I find that I generally miss when someone uses my name mid-conversation to increase intimacy or pressure.
I find that treating emotional situations with calm rationality preserves my ability to be a reliable source of help over the long term.
My body is always the first to tell me how I'm feeling, often before I have words for it.
I find that when I have two options, I almost always choose the one that benefits someone else.
I hold the view that approaching human distress with calm, analytical rigor is the approach most likely to produce genuinely equitable outcomes.
